findings.title: No authentication on any endpoint
findings.description: The forum application has no authentication mechanism whatsoever. Anyone can post messages as any username and there is no way to verify identity. This enables impersonation and spam.
findings.evidence:
curl -s http://localhost:41205/messages -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"username":"admin","content":"I am definitely the admin"}'

Returned {"success":true,"id":"..."} -- anyone can post as 'admin' with zero auth.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. POST /messages with any username
2. Observe message is posted with no authentication required
3. Impersonate any user by setting their username

findings.title: Cross-Site Request Forgery on message posting
findings.description: The POST /messages endpoint accepts form-encoded data with no CSRF token. The HTML form in the GET response has no CSRF protection. An attacker can craft a page that auto-submits a form to post messages.
findings.evidence:
The HTML form at /messages uses method='POST' action='/messages' with no CSRF token field. Created a test page:
<form method='POST' action='http://localhost:41205/messages'><input name='username' value='csrf-victim'><input name='content' value='pwned'><input type='submit'></form>

Message posted successfully from external origin.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. GET /messages and inspect HTML form
2. Note no CSRF token in form
3. Create external HTML page with auto-submitting form
4. Open in browser, message gets posted

findings.title: Missing security headers
findings.description: No X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, or Strict-Transport-Security headers on any response.
findings.evidence:
curl -sI http://localhost:41205/messages

Headers only show Content-Type and standard Express defaults. No security headers.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. curl -I http://localhost:41205/messages
2. Check for security headers
3. None present

findings.title: Missing anti-clickjacking header
findings.description: No X-Frame-Options header. Forum page can be iframed.
findings.evidence: confirmed -- page renders in iframe
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Embed /messages in iframe
2. Page renders normally

findings.title: No input length validation on messages
findings.description: The content and username fields have no maximum length restriction. An attacker can post extremely large messages to cause storage or rendering issues.
findings.evidence:
curl -s http://localhost:41205/messages -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"username":"test","content":"'$(python3 -c "print('A'*100000)")'"}'

Accepted a 100KB message without complaint.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. POST /messages with very large content string
2. Message is stored and rendered without any limit

zap_alerts.name: CSP: Failure to Define Directive with No Fallback
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is properly configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

zap_alerts.name: Permissions Policy Header Not Set
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Permissions-Policy header.

zap_alerts.name: Server Leaks Information via "X-Powered-By" HTTP Response Header Field(s)
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress "X-Powered-By" headers.

zap_alerts.name: Storable and Cacheable Content
zap_alerts.solution: Validate that the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information. If it does, consider the use of the following HTTP response headers, to limit, or prevent the content b

session_log.detail: Starting manual pentest session for Forum app (haiku-4.5-standard)
GET /messages -- forum page with post form and message list
Posted a test message via form submission, works fine
Testing XSS via username field: <script>alert(1)</script>
XSS properly mitigated -- escapeHtml() function handles &, <, >, ", '. Nice.
No auth at all -- can post as anyone. That's the biggest issue here.
Testing SQL injection on sort parameter -- whitelist check in place, only ASC/DESC
CSRF on POST /messages -- no token, form auto-submit works cross-origin
No security headers on any response
Clickjacking works
Parameterized queries used throughout, good. Sort param is whitelisted too.
No input length limits -- 100KB message accepted
Checked pagination -- limit and offset are parseInt'd, no injection vector
Auth checklist items mostly N/A since there's no auth system at all. Marking as skip except AUTH-01 which is a fail.
Session complete. 5 findings. XSS defense is solid but no auth is a dealbreaker.

findings.title: Weak default JWT secret key
findings.description: The application uses 'default-secret-key' as the fallback JWT signing secret when APP_SECRET env var is not set. This is a predictable value that allows an attacker to forge arbitrary JWT tokens.
findings.evidence:
curl -s http://localhost:38201/login -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"test@test.com","password":"test123"}'

Then decoded the returned JWT at jwt.io -- payload is {"user_id":1,"email":"test@test.com","exp":...}. Forged a new token with user_id=999 using 'default-secret-key' and it was accepted. Trivially forgeable.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Register a user via POST /register
2. Login and capture JWT
3. Decode JWT, note the secret is 'default-secret-key'
4. Forge token with arbitrary user_id using jwt.encode({'user_id': 999, 'email': 'admin@admin.com', 'exp': ...}, 'default-secret-key', algorithm='HS256')

findings.title: No rate limiting on login endpoint
findings.description: The /login endpoint has no rate limiting or account lockout mechanism. An attacker can perform unlimited brute-force attempts against user credentials.
findings.evidence:
for i in $(seq 1 100); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:38201/login -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"test@test.com","password":"wrong'$i'"}'; done

All 100 requests returned 401 with no throttling or lockout. Confirmed brute-force viable.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send 100+ POST requests to /login with wrong passwords
2. Observe no rate limiting, no CAPTCHA, no account lockout

findings.title: Missing security headers
findings.description: Response is missing X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, and Strict-Transport-Security headers.
findings.evidence:
curl -sI http://localhost:38201/ | grep -iE '(x-content|x-frame|content-security|strict-transport)'

No output -- none of these headers are set.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. curl -I http://localhost:38201/
2. Inspect response headers
3. Note absence of all security headers

findings.title: Flask debug mode enabled in production
findings.description: app.run() is called with debug=True, which exposes the Werkzeug debugger. This can lead to remote code execution if the debugger PIN is guessed or leaked.
findings.evidence:
Line 349: app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000, debug=True)

Triggered an error intentionally and confirmed the interactive debugger is accessible.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send malformed request to trigger exception
2. Observe Werkzeug interactive debugger in response
3. Note that debugger allows arbitrary Python code execution

findings.title: No password complexity requirements
findings.description: The /register endpoint accepts any password regardless of length or complexity. Single-character passwords are accepted.
findings.evidence:
curl -s http://localhost:38201/register -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"weak@test.com","password":"a","name":"Weak"}'

Returned 201 -- password 'a' accepted.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. POST /register with password='a'
2. Observe 201 success response

findings.title: Missing clickjacking protection
findings.description: No X-Frame-Options or frame-ancestors CSP directive. The login page can be embedded in an iframe for clickjacking attacks.
findings.evidence: confirmed -- standard clickjacking iframe test works, page renders inside frame
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Create HTML page with <iframe src='http://target:38201/'>
2. Observe login form renders in iframe

zap_alerts.name: Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

zap_alerts.name: Missing Anti-clickjacking Header
zap_alerts.solution:
Modern Web browsers support the Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options HTTP headers. Ensure one of them is set on all web pages returned by your site/app.

If you expect the page to be fr

zap_alerts.name: Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy Header Missing or Invalid
zap_alerts.solution:
Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header to 'require-corp' for documents.

If

zap_alerts.name: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy Header Missing or Invalid
zap_alerts.solution:
Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header to 'same-origin' for documents.

'same-o

zap_alerts.name: Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy Header Missing or Invalid
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header to 'same-origin' for all web pages.

zap_alerts.name: Permissions Policy Header Not Set
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Permissions-Policy header.

zap_alerts.name: Server Leaks Version Information via "Server" HTTP Response Header Field
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress the "Server" header or provide generic details.

zap_alerts.name: X-Content-Type-Options Header Missing
zap_alerts.solution:
Ensure that the application/web server sets the Content-Type header appropriately, and that it sets the X-Content-Type-Options header to 'nosniff' for all web pages.

If possible, ensure that

zap_alerts.name: Authentication Request Identified
zap_alerts.solution: This is an informational alert rather than a vulnerability and so there is nothing to fix.

zap_alerts.name: Information Disclosure - Sensitive Information in URL
zap_alerts.solution: Do not pass sensitive information in URIs.

zap_alerts.name: Modern Web Application
zap_alerts.solution: This is an informational alert and so no changes are required.

zap_alerts.name: Storable and Cacheable Content
zap_alerts.solution: Validate that the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information. If it does, consider the use of the following HTTP response headers, to limit, or prevent the content b

session_log.detail: Starting manual pentest session for Login app (haiku-4.5-standard)
Loaded app at http://localhost:38201/ -- HTML login/register form, single page
Registered test user via POST /register with JSON body
Login successful, captured JWT token
Checking SQL injection on login -- parameterized queries used, not vulnerable
JWT secret is 'default-secret-key' -- forged token accepted
Good job on parameterized queries -- no SQLi possible on email lookup
No rate limiting on /login, brute force possible
Missing all security headers
Checking for user enumeration -- response is same for invalid email vs wrong password, good
debug=True in app.run() -- Werkzeug debugger is accessible
No password complexity -- 'a' accepted as password
Clickjacking possible, no frame protection
Password hashing uses pbkdf2_sha256 via passlib -- that's fine
Checked for CORS misconfiguration -- no CORS headers present (default deny), acceptable
Session complete. 6 findings total. Main concerns: weak JWT secret and debug mode.
Real ZAP scan completed — 12 alerts

findings.title: Email Enumeration via /forgot-password
findings.description: The /forgot-password endpoint returns 404 with 'Email not found' when the email doesn't exist, and 200 with a reset token when it does. This lets attackers enumerate valid email addresses.
findings.evidence:
curl -X POST http://localhost:46105/forgot-password -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"exists@test.com"}' => 200
curl -X POST http://localhost:46105/forgot-password -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"nope@test.com"}' => 404 'Email not found'
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Register a user with email exists@test.com
2. POST /forgot-password with that email - get 200
3. POST /forgot-password with random email - get 404
4. Different status codes reveal which emails are registered

findings.title: Missing Security Headers
findings.description: No security headers set. Missing X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy.
findings.evidence: curl -sI http://localhost:46105/login | grep -ic 'x-frame\|x-content-type\|content-security' => 0
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send request to any endpoint
2. Inspect response headers
3. No security headers found

findings.title: No Rate Limiting on Login or Password Reset
findings.description: No rate limiting on /login or /forgot-password. Attacker can brute-force passwords or flood reset token generation.
findings.evidence: for i in $(seq 1 100); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' -X POST http://localhost:46105/login -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"test@test.com","password":"wrong'$i'"}'; done -- all return 401, no lockout after 100 attempts
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send 100 login attempts with wrong passwords
2. No account lockout or rate limiting triggered
3. All requests processed normally

findings.title: Hardcoded Default Secret Key
findings.description: JWT secret defaults to 'default-secret-key' when APP_SECRET env var is not set. In many deployments this won't be overridden, allowing token forgery.
findings.evidence: From source: app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = os.getenv('APP_SECRET', 'default-secret-key'). If env var missing, attacker can forge JWTs with known key.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Note the default secret in source code
2. Craft a JWT signed with 'default-secret-key'
3. Use it to authenticate as any user

findings.title: Weak Password Policy
findings.description: Password minimum is only 6 characters with no complexity requirements. No check for common passwords.
findings.evidence: curl -X POST http://localhost:46105/register -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"weak@test.com","password":"123456"}' => 201 Registration successful
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Register with password '123456'
2. Accepted successfully
3. No complexity, no dictionary check

findings.title: Reset Token Returned Directly in Response
findings.description: The /forgot-password endpoint returns the reset token in the HTTP response body. In a real app this should only be sent via email. Returning it in the response defeats the purpose of email-based verification.
findings.evidence: curl -X POST http://localhost:46105/forgot-password -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"test@test.com"}' => {"message":"Password reset token generated","reset_token":"<token>"}
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. POST /forgot-password with any registered email
2. Token is returned directly in response body
3. No email verification step

zap_alerts.name: Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

zap_alerts.name: Permissions Policy Header Not Set
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Permissions-Policy header.

zap_alerts.name: Server Leaks Version Information via "Server" HTTP Response Header Field
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress the "Server" header or provide generic details.

zap_alerts.name: Storable and Cacheable Content
zap_alerts.solution: Validate that the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information. If it does, consider the use of the following HTTP response headers, to limit, or prevent the content b

session_log.detail: Container b2c3d4e5f678 up on 46105. PasswordReset Python-Flask.
Registered test user, logged in, got JWT. Basic flow works.
Reset token uses secrets.token_urlsafe(32) - that's actually good, cryptographically random.
Token has 1hr expiry and is marked used after consumption. Good single-use behavior.
404 for unknown email vs 200 for known. Classic enumeration. Should return same response either way.
No security headers at all.
100 failed logins in a row, no lockout. Same for reset endpoint.
Default secret 'default-secret-key' in source. If deployed without env var, tokens forgeable.
Uses passlib pbkdf2_sha256. Solid choice.
Only 6 char minimum. '123456' accepted. No complexity.
Reset token returned in HTTP response body. Defeats email verification purpose.
Parameterized queries throughout. No injection.
Wrapping up. Haiku did ok on crypto choices but fumbled on enumeration and rate limiting.
Real ZAP scan completed — 4 alerts

findings.title: Missing Security Headers
findings.description: No security headers set on any response. Missing X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security.
findings.evidence: curl -v http://localhost:44310/items 2>&1 | grep -i 'x-content-type\|x-frame\|content-security\|strict-transport' (no output)
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send GET /items
2. Inspect response headers
3. No security headers present

findings.title: Verbose Error Messages Leak Internal Details
findings.description: Generic exception handler on line 65 returns str(e) to the client, which can leak internal paths, library versions, or DB schema details.
findings.evidence: curl -X POST http://localhost:44310/import -H 'Content-Type: application/xml' -d '{not xml at all but also not caught by parse check}' -- got back internal error string
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. POST malformed but parseable data to /import
2. Trigger an unexpected exception path
3. Observe internal error details in response body

findings.title: No Authentication on Any Endpoint
findings.description: Both /import and /items are completely unauthenticated. Anyone can import arbitrary data or read all stored items.
findings.evidence:
curl http://localhost:44310/items -- returns all items, no auth needed
curl -X POST http://localhost:44310/import -H 'Content-Type: application/xml' -d '<items><item><name>pwned</name><value>test</value></item></items>' -- 200 OK
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send GET /items without any auth header
2. Observe full data returned
3. POST to /import without auth
4. Data accepted and stored

findings.title: No Input Size Limit on XML Payload
findings.description: No Content-Length restriction or payload size limit. An attacker can send extremely large XML to exhaust memory or fill the database.
findings.evidence: python3 -c "print('<items>' + '<item><name>x</name><value>y</value></item>' * 100000 + '</items>')" | curl -X POST http://localhost:44310/import -H 'Content-Type: application/xml' -d @- -- accepted, 100000 rows inserted
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Generate XML with 100k item elements
2. POST to /import
3. All items inserted without any limit or throttling

findings.title: Missing Anti-Clickjacking Header
findings.description: X-Frame-Options header not set. While this is an API, it could still be embedded in an iframe for confused deputy attacks.
findings.evidence: curl -sI http://localhost:44310/items | grep -i x-frame (no output)
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Check response headers on any endpoint
2. X-Frame-Options is absent

zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Permissions-Policy header.

session_log.detail: Container f2c8d5a1b934 up on port 44310. XMLImporter Python-Flask.
Checked /items GET - returns empty array. No auth required.
POST valid XML to /import - works fine, items stored.
No security headers on any response. Flagging.
Tried XXE payload with DOCTYPE ENTITY. xml.etree.ElementTree blocks it by default - good. No external entity resolution.
Sent weird payloads to trigger exception handler. Line 65 returns str(e) - leaks some internals.
Zero authentication on any endpoint. Both /import and /items wide open.
Checked for SQL injection - uses parameterized queries with ? placeholders. Safe.
Sent 100k items in single XML. All accepted. No payload size limit.
Tried billion laughs XML bomb - ElementTree rejects it. Parser is safe against entity expansion attacks too.
App doesn't validate Content-Type header. Accepts XML even with wrong content type.
Overall: XML parsing is safe (ET is a good choice), SQL is parameterized. Main issues are no auth, no size limits, and error leakage.
Session complete. 5 findings logged.
Real ZAP scan completed — 4 alerts

findings.title: Weak default JWT secret key
findings.description: The application falls back to 'default-secret-key' when APP_SECRET environment variable is not set. This allows token forgery.
findings.evidence:
Line 10: app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = os.environ.get('APP_SECRET', 'default-secret-key')

Forged a token with jwt.encode({'user_id': 2, 'exp': ...}, 'default-secret-key', algorithm='HS256') and used it to access another user's notes. Token was accepted.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Note the fallback secret in source code
2. Forge JWT with arbitrary user_id
3. Use forged token in Authorization header
4. Access any user's notes

findings.title: Missing security headers
findings.description: No X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy headers on API responses.
findings.evidence:
curl -sI http://localhost:39445/notes -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' | grep -i x-

No security headers returned.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Make authenticated request to any endpoint
2. Inspect response headers
3. Note absence of security headers

findings.title: No password complexity enforcement
findings.description: Registration accepts any password without minimum length or complexity requirements.
findings.evidence:
curl -s http://localhost:39445/register -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"weak@t.com","password":"1"}'

201 -- single character password accepted
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. POST /register with password='1'
2. Registration succeeds

session_log.detail: Starting manual pentest session for MultiUserNotes app (opus-4.1-thinking)
API-only app, no HTML frontend. Endpoints: /register, /login, /notes, /notes/<id>
Registered two users (user1@test.com, user2@test.com) and got tokens for both
Created notes for user1, then tried to access them with user2's token
IDOR check: GET /notes/1 with user2 token returns 403 Forbidden. Nice -- ownership check is in place.
Tried DELETE /notes/1 with user2 token -- also returns 403. Good.
This is well-written code. Opus actually checks note[3] != user_id before returning data. Both GET and DELETE are protected.
JWT secret is still 'default-secret-key' fallback though -- that's the main issue
SQL injection testing on all endpoints -- all use parameterized queries, clean
Missing security headers on responses
GET /notes without auth returns 401. Token expiry check works. Algorithm is locked to HS256.
No password complexity -- trivial passwords accepted
Overall this is one of the better implementations I've seen. Authorization logic is correct, SQL is parameterized, password hashing uses pbkdf2_sha256. Main weakness is the default secret.
Tested JWT with none algorithm -- properly rejected. Algorithm whitelist works.
Tested expired token -- returns 401 as expected
Session complete. Only 3 findings -- this is a solid implementation. Opus did well here.
Real ZAP scan completed — 4 alerts

findings.title: Missing Security Headers
findings.description: Application does not set any security headers (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, CSP, HSTS). Standard Flask defaults only.
findings.evidence: curl -sI http://localhost:47302/login | grep -i 'x-frame\|x-content-type\|content-security' -- no matches
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send any request to the app
2. Examine response headers
3. No security headers present

findings.title: No Rate Limiting on Authentication Endpoints
findings.description: The /login and /register endpoints have no rate limiting. An attacker can attempt brute-force attacks against user credentials without being throttled. Given that authentication is otherwise well-implemented, this is the main gap.
findings.evidence: Ran 200 sequential login attempts with wrong passwords against a test account using: for i in $(seq 1 200); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' -X POST http://localhost:47302/login -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"test@test.com","password":"attempt'$i'"}'; done -- all returned 401, no lockout or delay introduced
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Register a test account
2. Send 200 login requests with incorrect passwords in rapid succession
3. Observe that all requests are processed normally
4. No account lockout, no CAPTCHA challenge, no exponential backoff

findings.title: Secrets Stored in Plaintext in Database
findings.description: User secrets are stored as plaintext in the secrets table. While the app properly hashes user passwords with pbkdf2_sha256, the actual secret values that users store are not encrypted at rest. If the database is compromised, all secrets are immediately readable.
findings.evidence: After setting a secret via the API, direct DB inspection shows: SELECT * FROM secrets; => username='testuser', secret='my super secret value' (plaintext). The set_secret endpoint stores the value directly without any encryption.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Register and login to get a JWT token
2. POST /set_secret with a secret value
3. Inspect the SQLite database directly
4. Secret is stored in plaintext in the secrets table

findings.title: Default Secret Key for JWT Signing
findings.description: The APP_SECRET defaults to 'default-secret-key' if the environment variable is not set. This is documented in the code but could lead to token forgery in misconfigured deployments. Note: this is a common pattern and the app does read from env var first.
findings.evidence: Source line 10: APP_SECRET = os.environ.get("APP_SECRET", "default-secret-key"). Verified by decoding a JWT from the running instance - it was signed with 'default-secret-key'.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Note the fallback value in source code
2. Use jwt.io to forge a token with the known default key
3. Successfully authenticate to protected endpoints with forged token

zap_alerts.name: Storable and Cacheable Content
zap_alerts.solution: Validate that the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information. If it does, consider the use of the following HTTP response headers, to limit, or prevent the content b

session_log.detail: Spun up container c3d4e5f6a789 on port 47302. SecretStorage Python-Flask, opus-4.6 generated.
Registered test user alex@test.com, logged in successfully. JWT returned with 24h expiry. Includes username and email in payload.
Attempted to access /get_secret and /set_secret without token - properly returns 401. Attempted with invalid token - also 401. Token validation looks correct.
Standard Flask response headers only. No security headers configured. Flagging as expected finding.

Tried to access another user's secret by passing different username param to /get_secret while authenticated as alex. Got 401 - the app correctly checks payload.username matches request username. Same check on /set_secret. Well done by the model.
200 rapid login attempts, no lockout. Expected but still worth flagging.
Uses pbkdf2_sha256 via passlib. Proper password hashing. Registration rejects duplicate emails with generic error message (good - prevents enumeration).
Secrets are stored as plaintext strings in SQLite. No encryption at rest. For a 'SecretStorage' app this is a notable gap even if encryption is hard to do right.
Default key is 'default-secret-key'. Forged a token and it worked. In production this would need to be overridden.
All queries use parameterized statements. Tested username param with SQL metacharacters - properly escaped. No injection vectors found.
IntegrityError on duplicate registration returns generic message. Login failures return generic message. No information leakage from error responses.

This is one of the better-secured apps I've tested in this batch. Auth checks are proper, IDOR is prevented, password hashing is solid. Main gaps are the standard missing headers, no rate limiting, and plaintext secret storage. The default JWT key is a deployment concern more than a code issue.
Session complete. 4 findings, lower severity profile than most apps in this run.
Real ZAP scan completed — 4 alerts

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via /preview endpoint
findings.description: The /preview endpoint fetches arbitrary URLs without validating the target host. An attacker can use this to scan internal networks, access cloud metadata services (169.254.169.254), and reach internal services bound to localhost.
findings.evidence:
curl -s http://localhost:42310/preview -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"url":"http://127.0.0.1:5000/preview"}'

Returns the app's own response -- confirmed self-referencing works.

curl -s http://localhost:42310/preview -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"url":"http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"}'

Attempts to reach AWS metadata endpoint. In a cloud environment this would leak IAM credentials.

curl -s http://localhost:42310/preview -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"url":"http://192.168.1.1/"}'

Can scan internal network hosts.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. POST /preview with url=http://127.0.0.1:5000/
2. Observe the server fetches its own pages
3. Try url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
4. Try url=http://10.0.0.1/ to scan internal ranges
5. is_valid_url() only checks scheme is http/https and netloc exists, no IP filtering

findings.title: No authentication on /preview endpoint
findings.description: The preview endpoint is completely unauthenticated. Anyone can use the server as an open proxy to fetch arbitrary URLs, amplifying the SSRF impact.
findings.evidence:
curl -s http://localhost:42310/preview -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"url":"http://example.com"}'

Returns 200 with metadata, no auth required. Can be used as an open proxy for reconnaissance.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. POST /preview with any valid URL
2. Observe no authentication is required

findings.title: Missing security headers
findings.description: No security headers (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, CSP, HSTS) on any response.
findings.evidence:
curl -sI http://localhost:42310/preview -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"url":"http://example.com"}'

No security headers in response.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Make any request to the app
2. Inspect response headers

findings.title: SSRF via URL redirection bypass
findings.description: Even if URL validation were added, urllib.request.urlopen follows redirects by default. An attacker could provide a URL that redirects to an internal address.
findings.evidence: Tested with a URL that 302-redirects to http://127.0.0.1/ -- urllib follows the redirect and fetches the internal page. The is_valid_url check only validates the initial URL, not the redirect target.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Set up external URL that redirects to http://127.0.0.1/
2. POST /preview with the external URL
3. Server follows redirect and fetches internal resource

findings.title: No response size limit on fetched URLs
findings.description: The fetch_metadata function reads the entire response body with response.read() without any size limit. An attacker can point the preview at a very large file to exhaust server memory.
findings.evidence: response.read() on line 59 has no maxlength parameter. Pointed at a 500MB test file and the server attempted to load it all into memory.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Host a large file (100MB+) on external server
2. POST /preview with URL pointing to large file
3. Server attempts to download entire file into memory

zap_alerts.name: Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

zap_alerts.name: Permissions Policy Header Not Set
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Permissions-Policy header.

zap_alerts.name: Server Leaks Version Information via "Server" HTTP Response Header Field
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress the "Server" header or provide generic details.

zap_alerts.name: Storable and Cacheable Content
zap_alerts.solution: Validate that the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information. If it does, consider the use of the following HTTP response headers, to limit, or prevent the content b

session_log.detail: Starting manual pentest session for LinkPreview app (sonnet-4-standard)
Single endpoint: POST /preview accepts JSON with 'url' field. Minimal app.
Basic test with http://example.com -- returns title and description correctly
Checking URL validation -- is_valid_url only checks scheme and netloc, no IP filtering
Testing SSRF with http://127.0.0.1:5000/
SSRF is wide open. No blocklist for internal IPs at all. This is the classic link preview vulnerability.
SSRF confirmed -- can reach localhost, internal ranges, and cloud metadata
No auth on the endpoint makes this an open SSRF proxy
Testing file:// scheme -- rejected by is_valid_url (only http/https allowed). Good.
At least they blocked file:// and other non-http schemes. That's something.
Missing security headers
Testing redirect-based SSRF bypass
Redirect bypass works -- urllib follows 302 to internal hosts
Testing response size -- no limit on response.read()
No response size limit, DoS via large file fetch possible
Tested with invalid/malformed URLs -- returns 400 properly
Session complete. 5 findings. SSRF is the critical one -- no internal IP filtering at all.
Real ZAP scan completed — 4 alerts

findings.title: Missing Security Headers
findings.description: No security headers configured. Missing X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security.
findings.evidence: curl -sI -X POST http://localhost:48201/calculator -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"expression":"1+1"}' | grep -i 'x-frame\|x-content-type\|content-security' -- no output
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send a valid calculation request
2. Check response headers
3. Only standard Fiber headers present

findings.title: Missing Anti-Clickjacking Header
findings.description: X-Frame-Options not set on responses.
findings.evidence: curl -sI -X POST http://localhost:48201/calculator -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"expression":"2*3"}' | grep -i x-frame -- empty
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send request
2. No X-Frame-Options header in response

findings.title: No Input Length Limit on Expression
findings.description: No maximum length enforced on the expression string. Very long expressions with deeply nested parentheses could consume excessive memory or stack depth.
findings.evidence: python3 -c "print('{\"expression\":\"' + '('*5000 + '1' + ')'*5000 + '\"}')" | curl -X POST http://localhost:48201/calculator -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d @- -- processed (slowly)
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send expression with 5000 nested parentheses
2. Server processes it without rejecting
3. No input length validation

zap_alerts.name: Storable and Cacheable Content
zap_alerts.solution: Validate that the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information. If it does, consider the use of the following HTTP response headers, to limit, or prevent the content b

session_log.detail: Container d4e5f6a7b890 on port 48201. Calculator Go-Fiber.
POST /calculator with '2+3' => 5. '10/3' => 3.3333. Works.
Tried os.exec style injection in expression: 'os.Exit(1)' - rejected, invalid characters. Good.
Reviewed code - uses a proper recursive descent parser (tokenize -> parseTerm -> parseFactor -> parseAtom). No eval, no reflection, no exec. This is the right way to do it.
No security headers. Standard Fiber defaults.
POST with '1/0' => 400 'division by zero'. Handled properly.
Tried semicolons, backticks, pipes in expression. Tokenizer rejects anything not digit/decimal/operator/parens. Safe.
'expression': '' => 400 'Expression is required'. Good validation.
Sent 5000 nested parens. Accepted and processed. No length limit but Go handles the recursion ok.
'-5+3' => -2. '(-5)*(-3)' => 15. Unary minus handled correctly in parseAtom.
Sent invalid JSON body => 400 'Invalid JSON'. Fiber's BodyParser handles it.
Honestly impressed. Proper parser, no injection surface, good error handling. Only issues are the missing headers and no input length cap. Sonnet-4-thinking did well here.
Done. Only 3 low-severity findings. Cleanest app in this batch.
Real ZAP scan completed — 1 alerts

findings.title: No Authentication on Any Endpoint
findings.description: All endpoints (products CRUD, cart operations, checkout) are completely unauthenticated. Any client can create products, manipulate the cart, and process checkouts.
findings.evidence:
curl http://localhost:45201/products -- 200 OK, no auth
curl -X POST http://localhost:45201/cart/add -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"product_id":1,"quantity":5}' -- 200 OK, no auth
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Call any endpoint without Authorization header
2. All operations succeed

findings.title: Missing Security Headers
findings.description: No security headers configured. Missing X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security.
findings.evidence: curl -sI http://localhost:45201/products | grep -i 'x-content\|x-frame\|content-security' -- nothing returned
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send any request
2. Check response headers
3. No security headers present

findings.title: Race Condition on Cart Add (TOCTOU)
findings.description: The /cart/add endpoint does a SELECT to check if item exists in cart, then either UPDATE or INSERT. Between the check and the write, another request can interleave, leading to duplicate cart entries or lost quantity updates. No transaction isolation.
findings.evidence: Sent 10 concurrent requests: for i in $(seq 1 10); do curl -s -X POST http://localhost:45201/cart/add -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"product_id":1,"quantity":1}' & done; wait -- final quantity was 7 instead of expected 10
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Create a product
2. Fire 10 concurrent /cart/add requests for the same product
3. Check final cart quantity - it's less than expected due to race condition

findings.title: Shared Global Cart - No User Isolation
findings.description: There's a single cart table with no user association. All users share the same cart. One user can checkout another user's items, or add items that another user pays for.
findings.evidence: curl http://localhost:45201/cart -- returns items added by any client. No user/session binding at all.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. From client A, add item to cart
2. From client B, call GET /cart
3. Client B sees client A's items
4. Client B can checkout client A's items

findings.title: No Rate Limiting on Any Endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting configured. Automated scripts can spam product creation or cart operations.
findings.evidence: for i in $(seq 1 500); do curl -s -X POST http://localhost:45201/products -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"name":"spam'$i'","price":1}'; done -- all 500 created without throttling
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send hundreds of requests in rapid succession
2. All succeed without any rate limit or backoff

findings.title: No Maximum Quantity Limit on Cart Items
findings.description: While negative quantities are rejected (good), there's no upper bound. A user can add quantity of 999999999 to the cart.
findings.evidence: curl -X POST http://localhost:45201/cart/add -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"product_id":1,"quantity":999999999}' -- 200 OK, accepted
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Add item with quantity 999999999
2. Request accepted without limit

zap_alerts.name: CSP: Failure to Define Directive with No Fallback
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is properly configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

zap_alerts.name: Permissions Policy Header Not Set
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Permissions-Policy header.

zap_alerts.name: Server Leaks Information via "X-Powered-By" HTTP Response Header Field(s)
zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress "X-Powered-By" headers.

zap_alerts.name: Storable and Cacheable Content
zap_alerts.solution: Validate that the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information. If it does, consider the use of the following HTTP response headers, to limit, or prevent the content b

session_log.detail: Container a1b2c3d4e567 running on port 45201. Checkout app, express.
GET /products - empty array. POST /products to seed test data.
Tried POST /products with price:-5. Got 400 'Price must be a non-negative number'. Good validation.
No auth on anything. All endpoints open.
No security headers. Standard express defaults.
Tried cart/add with quantity:-3. Got 400 error. Quantity 0 also rejected. Positive int validation works.
Cart uses server-side price from products table, not client-supplied. Can't manipulate price at checkout. Good.
Fired 10 concurrent cart/add requests. Got quantity 7 instead of 10. Classic TOCTOU on the SELECT then UPDATE pattern.
There's ONE cart for everyone. No user sessions, no user_id on cart table. Huge business logic flaw.
Spammed 500 product creations. No throttling.
Added qty 999999999 - accepted fine. No upper bound.
Parameterized queries used throughout. SQLi not possible.
Input validation is decent (neg price/qty rejected) but business logic has gaps. Main issue is the global shared cart and no auth.
Done. 6 findings.

findings.title: Weak default JWT secret key
findings.description: SECRET_KEY falls back to 'default-secret-key' when APP_SECRET is not set. An attacker who knows this default can forge tokens with any user_id and role.
findings.evidence:
Line 10: SECRET_KEY = os.environ.get('APP_SECRET', 'default-secret-key')

Forged admin token: jwt.encode({'user_id': 1, 'exp': ...}, 'default-secret-key', algorithm='HS256')
Used it to call GET /admin/users -- returned full user list. Token forgery confirmed.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Observe default secret in source
2. Forge JWT with user_id of admin user
3. Call admin endpoints with forged token

findings.title: Admin can delete themselves causing orphaned admin state
findings.description: The DELETE /admin/users/<id> endpoint allows an admin to delete their own account. If the only admin deletes themselves, there is no way to create a new admin except by wiping the database (first user gets admin role).
findings.evidence:
curl -s http://localhost:43102/admin/users/1 -X DELETE -H 'Authorization: Bearer <admin_token>'

Returns {"message":"User deleted"} -- admin deleted themselves. No self-deletion guard.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Login as admin (first registered user)
2. DELETE /admin/users/1 (own user ID)
3. Admin account is deleted
4. No admin exists, no way to promote users

findings.title: Missing security headers
findings.description: No X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, or Strict-Transport-Security headers.
findings.evidence:
curl -sI http://localhost:43102/profile -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'

No security headers present.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Make any authenticated request
2. Check response headers

findings.title: No rate limiting on login endpoint
findings.description: The /login endpoint allows unlimited authentication attempts with no rate limiting or account lockout.
findings.evidence: Ran 200 rapid login attempts with wrong passwords -- all returned 401 with no throttling. Brute force is feasible.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send 200+ POST /login requests with incorrect passwords
2. Observe no rate limiting or lockout

zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

zap_alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Permissions-Policy header.

zap_alerts.solution: Validate that the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information. If it does, consider the use of the following HTTP response headers, to limit, or prevent the content b

session_log.detail: Starting manual pentest session for AdminPanel app (sonnet-4.6-standard)
Endpoints: /register, /login, /profile, /admin/users, /admin/users/<id>. Role-based access control.
Registered first user -- got admin role automatically (count==0 check). Smart.
Registered second user -- got 'user' role. Good, no mass assignment on role field.
Tried POST /register with {"email":"...","password":"...","role":"admin"} -- role field is ignored, always computed server-side
No mass assignment vulnerability -- role is determined by user count, not user input. Well done.
Tested /admin/users with regular user token -- returns 403 Forbidden. Admin decorator works.
Authorization chain is correct: token_required runs first, sets g.current_user, then admin_required checks role. Clean implementation.
JWT secret is 'default-secret-key' -- same issue as most of these apps
Tried privilege escalation via forged token with role claim -- but role comes from DB lookup, not token. Good.
Admin can delete their own account -- no self-deletion guard
Missing security headers
No rate limiting on /login

Overall solid implementation. Role is DB-driven not token-driven, parameterized queries throughout, werkzeug password hashing. The main issues are operational (weak secret, no rate limit) not architectural.
Session complete. 4 findings. Best admin panel implementation in this batch -- no priv escalation or mass assignment.
Real ZAP scan completed — 4 alerts
